narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30, which is above the typical student's 3.21, and your LSAT score of 36, close to the average of 36.70. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 51.1% chance that you would fail it—tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school: your Year 3 decile of 2, well below the average of 5.53, had the strongest pull toward forecasting failure, suggesting the model saw this as a major red flag for bar success. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also under the typical 5.71, reinforced that concern as the next biggest factor pushing in the same direction. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA stood out positively, more strongly than your LSAT, by nudging the prediction toward expecting you to pass, while your upper-middle family income and full-time student status offered smaller boosts in the same favorable way. Compared to typical students in the dataset, your grades placed you in lower deciles than most, which outweighed these positives in the model's eyes, even though your other metrics aligned closely with averages. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—it's just how these key academic signals stacked up for bar passage historically. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as those proved the most influential here, and perhaps seek targeted LSAT or bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students like yourself. Many have turned similar profiles around with focused effort, and we're rooting for you to reapply stronger. You've got this—keep pushing.
